query: clinical guidelines for managing hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state vs diabetic ketoacidosis

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pre-Diabetes and diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Once diabetes mellitus is identified, patients should be assessed for complications, and pharmacologic therapy should be started. Microvascular complications of diabetes include retinopathy, neuropathy, and diabetic kidney disease (DKD). Macrovascular complication includes atherosclerosis (predisposing to stroke, coronary artery disease, and peripheral arterial disease). Intensive glycemic control leads to reductions in both microvascular and macrovascular outcomes. Hyperglycemic complications include diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperglycemic hyperosmolar syndrome. Any pharmacologic therapy for diabetes should ideally be coupled with lifestyle interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Treatment / Management -- Choice of Fluids
retrieved_docs.content: Isotonic fluids have been well established for more than 50 years as preferred fluids. Colloids vs. crystalloids were compared for critically ill patients, in a 2013 meta-analysis, and crystalloid was found to be non-inferior. [24] Traditionally, 0.9% normal saline has been used. There has been a concern that normal saline may contribute to hyperchloremia and hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis; however, this typically occurs when it is used for large volumes. There have been small studies comparing normal saline with other solutions like Ringer lactate. These studies did not show differences in clinical outcomes. [25] [26] [27] Normal saline continues to be used for initial hydration.

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Treatment / Management -- Bicarbonate
retrieved_docs.content: Bicarbonate replacement does not appear to be beneficial. In one study, the difference in time to resolution of acidosis (8 hours vs. 8 hours; p = 0.7) and time to hospital discharge (68 hours vs. 61 hours; p = 0.3) was found to be statistically insignificant between patients who received intravenous bicarbonate (n = 44) compared with those who did not (n = 42). [38] In another pediatric study, children with diabetic ketoacidosis who have low PaCO2 and high BUN concentrations at presentation and those treated with bicarbonate were at increased risk for cerebral edema. [39] Proposed pitfalls of the use of sodium bicarbonate therapy in DKA may include paradoxical CSF acidosis, hypokalemia, large sodium bolus, and cerebral edema. However, it may be used in patients with severe acidemia. The most recent ADA guidelines do recommend the use of sodium bicarbonate therapy in patients with pH less than 7.1. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: According to the recommendation of the American Diabetic Association and current international guidelines, HHS is defined by plasma glucose level greater than 600 mg/dL, plasma effective osmolarity greater than 320 mOsm/L, and absence of significant ketoacidosis. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the difference between diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperosmolar hyperglycemic nonketotic syndrome. Outline the prevalence of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic nonketotic syndrome in type 2 diabetes. Describe the evaluation of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic nonketotic syndrome. Summarize the role of the interprofessional team in the management of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic nonketotic syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Euglycemic Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium should also be carefully monitored as total body potassium levels are likely to be depleted, and IV supplementation of potassium and other electrolytes may be needed. Blood glucose levels should be checked hourly, and electrolytes should be checked every 4 hours at a minimum, as is the standard for treating DKA. Patients taking SGLT2 inhibitors should have these medications discontinued as soon as the diagnosis is recognized and held until recovery from the acute illness. [10] Sodium bicarbonate infusions are not indicated and even use in severe acidemia of pH less than 6.9 is controversial. Patients generally require ICU admission for close hemodynamic and laboratory monitoring and frequent titration of insulin infusion. [2] [36] [37] Treatment with IV fluid resuscitation should continue until the anion gap closes and acidosis has resolved.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Intraoperative Management -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: There are no strict guidelines for canceling a surgical case due to hyperglycemia. The Society for Ambulatory Anesthesia (SAMBA) recommends canceling a case if a patient is experiencing diabetic ketoacidosis, hyperglycemic hyperosmolar nonketotic state, or severe dehydration. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Euglycemic Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Vigilant monitoring of capillary or urine ketones by patients with diabetes, especially during episodes of nausea or illness, can diagnose EDKA before it becomes severe. Although the SGLT2 inhibitors have been shown to have extra benefits on cardiovascular and kidney health, they are not recommended to be used in managing patients with T1DM because of the high risk of EDKA. [41] [42] [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Euglycemic Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initial management should be directed toward fluid resuscitation, as patients usually present as profoundly dehydrated. Begin with the administration of isotonic saline or lactated Ringer solution. The American Diabetes Association (ADA) recommends 1 to 1.5 L/hr isotonic fluids during the first 1 to 2 hours. Continuous insulin infusion should follow fluid replacement, contingent on serum potassium levels greater than 3.3 mEq/L, starting at a rate of 0.05 to 0.1 U/kg/hr. In contrast to DKA management, since serum glucose in EDKA is less than 250 mg/dL, dextrose 5% should initially be added to the fluids to avoid hypoglycemia and hasten clearance of ketosis. [34] Consider increasing the amount of dextrose to 10% if ketoacidosis persists on D5%. [27] [35] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin -- Indications -- Inpatient Indications for Insulin
retrieved_docs.content: Management of diabetes mellitus patients in the hospital: Glycemic control in patients with type 1 and type 2 diabetes admitted to general wards for conditions other than diabetes is achievable with subcutaneous or intravenous insulin infusion. This therapeutic approach is to avoid hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, volume depletion, or electrolyte abnormalities during hospitalization. [10] Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and hyperosmolar hyperglycemia (HHS) are complications of type 1 and type 2 diabetes, respectively, and both represent extremes in hyperglycemia. Insulin is indicated in both DKA and HHS to normalize blood glucose levels. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team, including social workers, are often needed to address these particular situations. Meticulous discharge planning, involving social workers for patients with socioeconomic needs, and hospital initiated follow up clinics for discharged patients are some of the factors important to reduce the recurrences of DKA in the same individual. Finally, patient education is highly recommended, as in many cases, the cause of DKA is failing to comply with treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Immediate blood work is necessary to determine the state of ketoacidosis, and imaging may be necessary to rule out pneumonia. If the mental status is altered, a CT scan may be required, and thus the radiologist must be notified about the patient's hemodynamic status. No patient with DKA should go unmonitored to a radiology suite. The infectious disease expert and cardiologist should be consulted if there is suspicion of infection or MI as the trigger.

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Treatment / Management -- Infusion Rate
retrieved_docs.content: Infusion of 15-20 ml per Kg body weight in the first 1 hour is typically appropriate. Aggressive hydration with 1 liter/hour for 4 hours has been compared in a study to a slower rate of hydration at half the rate. Slower hydration was found to be equally effective. [28] However, in critically ill patients, including those with hypotension, aggressive fluid therapy is preferred. There has been extensive debate regarding the risk of cerebral edema in patients with aggressive early volume resuscitation. There are studies that have demonstrated rates of increased cerebral edema with aggressive volume, particularly in the pediatric population. Yet other studies show no difference in outcome and theorize that patients at greatest risk from cerebral edema present at a later stage and are the most severe volume depleted. [29] Maintainance:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2858
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is classified as a fluid and electrolyte disorder. Fluid and electrolyte disorders are linked to غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري (diabetic hyperosmolar coma), indicating that disturbances in sodium concentration and overall osmolar balance can be associated with severe hyperglycemic states that lead to coma. Recognizing the connection between acute hypernatremia and غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري helps clinicians monitor and manage patients with significant electrolyte and osmolar abnormalities to prevent progression to life‑threatening coma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى (renal failure), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals suffering from kidney failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1597
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that include electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات) as a specific subset. Electrolyte imbalance, in turn, comprises conditions such as increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية) and hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم). Additionally, fluid and electrolyte disorders are linked to diabetic hyperosmolar coma (غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري), underscoring the critical role of precise fluid and electrolyte management in patients with severe hyperglycemia. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians identify and treat related metabolic abnormalities more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2614
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is described as a general clinical state that is interpreted by clinicians (is_interpreted_by) and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications. This indicates that an uncontrolled disease condition determination reflects a concerning overall clinical status and frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with such uncontrolled conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia and hyperosmolality are classified within fluid, electrolyte and acid‑base disorders, a subset of metabolic disorders that intersect with other endocrine/metabolic/nutritional diseases and carbohydrate metabolism disorder. These conditions are linked to plasma hyperosmolality and severe hyperosmolar states such as diabetic hyperosmolar coma, and they commonly coexist with electrolyte abnormalities including hyperkalemia, increased anion gap, hyperuricemia, hypochloremia, and with acid‑base disturbances such as acidosis and respiratory alkalosis. High plasma osmolality is closely associated with hyperglycemia, hyperketonemia, and both type 1 diabetes and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, which are treated by targeting the INSR protein with rapid‑acting insulin analogues such as insulin aspart; however, insulin aspart is contraindicated in hypoglycemia. Endocrine pancreatic disease manifestations include hyperglycemia, elevated blood ketones, hyperinsulinemia,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia and hyperosmolality are classified as fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base imbalances and intersect with diabetic hyperosmolar coma, highlighting the link between severe electrolyte disturbance and uncontrolled diabetes. Within the hierarchy “Diseases of the endocrine pancreas including diabetes” (a subset of other endocrine disorders) the entities include elevated blood glucose, increased blood ketones in diabetic patients, diabetic hyperosmolar coma, diabetes without complications, type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, hypoglycemia, disorders of gastrin secretion, hyperinsulinemia, and glucagon‑producing pancreatic tumor (glucagonoma). Insulin aspart (human) binds the INSR protein and is used therapeutically for type 1 and type 2 diabetes, but is contraindicated in hypoglycemia to avoid further glucose lowering. High plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose and increased ketone levels, and occurs in both type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Elevated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with high blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم), indicating that hyperglycemia often contributes to or results from elevated osmotic concentration in the blood. This condition is also linked to urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that disturbances of the urinary system can be an underlying or related factor in the development of high plasma osmolality. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the close interplay between osmotic balance, fluid volume status, and glucose regulation. Recognizing that hyperglycemia, urinary tract pathology, and hypovolemia commonly intersect with elevated plasma osmolality helps clinicians assess underlying causes and guide comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders are closely linked to the development of diabetic hyperosmolar coma, as disturbances in fluid balance and electrolyte concentrations can precipitate the severe hyperosmolar state that characterizes this life‑threatening complication of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2606
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is categorized under other endocrine disorders, indicating that it is considered a component of the broader group of endocrine system diseases. Clinically, hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is associated with hypoglycemia, suggesting that patients who experience low blood‑sugar levels may also develop elevated serum potassium. In addition, hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض), reflecting a common electrolyte and acid‑base disturbance that can accompany high potassium levels. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of evaluating electrolyte balance and acid‑base status when managing endocrine disorders such as hypoglycemia, as concurrent hyperkalemia and acidosis may influence diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic decisions.
